title: Howl's Moving Castle - Chapter Eight: In which Sophie leaves the castle in several directions at once (Fragment 4)
text:
Sophie‚Äôs heart pounded a bit as they walked across the common to Mrs. Fairfax‚Äôs house, but only in the way hearts do when you have done a lot rather quickly. She felt very grateful for whatever Howl and Calcifer had done.

‚ÄúNice place,‚Äù Michael remarked as he hid the boots in Mrs. Fairfax‚Äôs hedge.

Sophie agreed. The house was the biggest in the village. It was thatched, with white walls between the black beams, and, as Sophie remembered from visits as a child, you walked up to the porch through a garden crowded with flowers and humming with bees. Over the porch a honeysuckle and a white climbing rose were competing as to which could give most work to the bees. It was a perfect, hot summer morning down here in Upper Folding.

Mrs. Fairfax answered the door herself. She was one of those plump, comfortable ladies, with swathes of butter-colored hair coiled round her head, who made you feel good with life just to look at her. Sophie felt just the tiniest bit envious of Lettie. Mrs. Fairfax looked from Sophie to Michael. She had seen Sophie last a year ago as a girl of seventeen, and there was no reason for her to recognize her as an old woman of ninety. ‚ÄúGood morning to you,‚Äù she said politely.

Sophie sighed. Michael said, ‚ÄúThis is Lettie Hatter‚Äôs great-aunt. I brought her here to see Lettie.‚Äù

‚ÄúOh, I thought the face looked familiar!‚Äù Mrs. Fairfax exclaimed. ‚ÄúThere‚Äôs quite a family likeness. Do come in. Lettie‚Äôs a little bit busy just now, but have some scones and honey while you wait.‚Äù

She opened her front door wider. Instantly a large collie dog squeezed past Mrs. Fairfax‚Äôs skirts, barged between Sophie and Michael, and ran across the nearest flower bed, snapping off flowers right and left.

‚ÄúOh, stop him!‚Äù Mrs. Fairfax gasped, flying off in pursuit. ‚ÄúI don‚Äôt want him out just now!‚Äù

There was a minute or so of helter-skelter chase, in which the dog ran hither and thither, whining in a disturbed way, and Mrs. Fairfax and Sophie ran after the dog, jumping flower beds and getting in one another‚Äôs way, and Michael ran after Sophie crying, ‚ÄúStop! You‚Äôll make yourself ill!‚Äù Then the dog set off loping round one corner of the house. Michael realized that the way to stop Sophie was to stop the dog. He made a crosswise dash through the flower beds, plunged round the house after the dog, and seized it by two handfuls of its thick coat just as it reached the orchard at the back.

Sophie hobbled up to find Michael pulling the dog away backward and making such strange faces at her that she thought at first he was ill. But he jerked his head so often toward the orchard that she realized he was only trying to tell her something. She stuck her face round the corner of the house, expecting to see a swarm of bees.

Howl was there with Lettie. They were in a grove of mossy apple trees in full bloom, with a row of beehives in the distance. Lettie sat in a white garden seat. Howl was kneeling on one knee in the grass at her feet, holding one of her hands and looking noble and ardent. Lettie was smiling lovingly at him. But the worst of it, as far as Sophie was concerned, was that Lettie did not look like Martha at all. She was her own extremely beautiful self. She was wearing a dress of the same kind of pinks and white as the crowded apple blossom overhead. Her dark hair trailed in glossy curls over one shoulder and her eyes shone with devotion for Howl.

meta.author: Diana Wynne Jones
meta.sectionTitle: Chapter Eight: In which Sophie leaves the castle in several directions at once

vocabulary.definition: very enthusiastic or passionate
vocabulary.example: Howl was looking noble and ardent.

vocabulary.definition: an act of running after someone or something to catch them
vocabulary.example: Mrs. Fairfax flew off in pursuit during the chase.

vocabulary.definition: a rose plant that grows by climbing up a support or wall
vocabulary.example: A white climbing rose was competing as to which could give most work to the bees.

vocabulary.definition: striving to gain or win something by defeating others
vocabulary.example: A honeysuckle and a white climbing rose were competing to attract bees.

vocabulary.definition: love, loyalty, or enthusiasm for a person, activity, or cause
vocabulary.example: Her eyes shone with devotion for Howl.

vocabulary.definition: feeling upset or troubled
vocabulary.example: The dog was whining in a disturbed way.

vocabulary.definition: feeling a desire to have what someone else has
vocabulary.example: Sophie felt just the tiniest bit envious of Lettie.

vocabulary.definition: a resemblance between family members
vocabulary.example: There‚Äôs quite a family likeness.

vocabulary.definition: to breathe in suddenly with surprise or pain
vocabulary.example: Mrs. Fairfax gasped, flying off in pursuit.

vocabulary.definition: smooth and shiny
vocabulary.example: Her dark hair trailed in glossy curls over one shoulder.

vocabulary.definition: feeling or showing thanks because of something kind that someone has done
vocabulary.example: She felt very grateful for whatever Howl and Calcifer had done.

vocabulary.definition: in a disorderly or hurried way
vocabulary.example: There was a minute or so of helter-skelter chase.

vocabulary.definition: walked with difficulty, typically because of injury
vocabulary.example: Sophie hobbled up to find Michael pulling the dog away.

vocabulary.definition: a plant with sweet-smelling flowers that attract bees
vocabulary.example: Over the porch a honeysuckle and a white climbing rose were competing.

vocabulary.definition: having or showing fine personal qualities or high moral principles
vocabulary.example: Howl was looking noble and ardent.

vocabulary.definition: a piece of land where fruit trees are grown
vocabulary.example: The dog reached the orchard at the back of the house.

vocabulary.definition: having a full, rounded shape
vocabulary.example: She was one of those plump, comfortable ladies.

vocabulary.definition: (of the heart) to beat strongly and quickly, especially because of excitement or nervousness
vocabulary.example: Sophie‚Äôs heart pounded a bit as they walked across the common.

vocabulary.definition: to identify someone or something seen before
vocabulary.example: There was no reason for Mrs. Fairfax to recognize her as an old woman.

vocabulary.definition: a small round sweet cake, often eaten with tea
vocabulary.example: Have some scones and honey while you wait.

vocabulary.definition: took hold of suddenly and forcibly
vocabulary.example: He seized the dog by two handfuls of its thick coat.

vocabulary.definition: a broad strip or area of something, used figuratively for large amounts of hair or fabric
vocabulary.example: Swathes of butter-colored hair coiled round her head.

vocabulary.definition: (of a roof) covered with straw or a similar material
vocabulary.example: The house was thatched, with white walls between the black beams.

vocabulary.definition: (of a plant or hair) growing or hanging down loosely
vocabulary.example: Her dark hair trailed in glossy curls over one shoulder.

vocabulary.definition: making low, complaining sounds
vocabulary.example: The dog was whining in a disturbed way.
